sanskrit: प्रशान्तमनसं ह्येनं योगिनं सुखमुत्तमम्। उपैति शान्तरजसं ब्रह्मभूतमकल्मषम्॥
transliteration: praśānta-manasaṃ hy enaṃ yoginaṃ sukham uttamam | upaiti śāntarajasaṃ brahmabhūtam akalmaṣam ||
literal_meaning: To the one whose mind is completely stilled—this yogī—the highest happiness comes; he is of pacified rajas, become Brahman, stainless.
contextual_meaning: When attention is no longer hijacked by compulsive thought or craving, the nervous system shifts from threat-mode to open presence; the self-sense is no longer an anxious project but the silent field in which all experience arises. This is the ‘highest happiness’ because it is not contingent on objects or outcomes.
psychological_state: Post-absorptive integration: the default-mode network is down-regulated, the inner critic is silent, and the self is experienced as spacious, benevolent awareness.
trigger_scenario: You finish a 10-day mindfulness retreat; your phone is off, the inner chatter has thinned to a whisper, and you step outside to feel the breeze as if it were the first breeze in the world.
anchor_text: Rest in stillness; the quiet mind knows unshakable joy.
